W2SBNγ is a function that takes relu(W1x + b1) and relu(W1xi + b1) as inputs and applies a specified operation involving gamma parameter and weight matrices W1, W2. It involves a simplified BatchNorm (SBN) operation that helps in the normalization of the data.
W2SBNγ uses relu activation function to transform the input before performing BatchNorm operation. The relu function introduces non-linearity in the network.
W2SBNγ considers relu(W1xi + b1) as part of its operation, indicating that the weight matrix W1 is applied to the input features xi for processing.
W1xi undergoes a relu activation function to introduce non-linearity in the computation process. This step plays a role in transforming the input features before further processing.

W2-MLP-Sim-BN is represented as a function f sbn in the expression. It involves a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) with a simplified BatchNorm operation. This architecture is designed to perform specific computations efficiently.
W2-MLP-Sim-BN includes the weight matrix W2 in its calculations, indicating that the matrix is crucial for the operations performed by this architecture.

The weight matrices W1 and W2 are used in conjunction with other parameters such as biases and activation functions to build the neural network. These matrices play a significant role in defining the architecture of the network.

The parameter θ, which comprises the weight matrices W1, plays a pivotal role in the operations involved in processing the input data. The values of θ influence the behavior of the network during training and inference.

The parameter θ consists of biases represented by b1. Biases are essential components in neural networks as they help in adjusting the output along with the weighted inputs. The values of biases impact the overall performance of the model.
Model fθ interpolates the dataset {(xi, yi)}n ∀i, fθ(xi) = yi.
Our theoretical analysis focuses on understanding the sharpness of the trained models.
Precisely, for a model fθ parameterized by θ, a dataset {(xi, yi)}n i=1 and loss function ℓ, we will use the trace of Hessian of loss function, Tr(∇2L(θ)) to measure how sharp the loss is at θ.
We train a 2-layer MLP with ReLU activation without bias using gradient descent with weight decay and 1-SAM on Pxor with dimension d = 30 and training set size n = 100.
In both cases, the model reaches perfect generalization.

Although weight decay doesn’t explicitly regularize model sharpness, the flatness of the model decreases through training, which is consistent with our Lemma 3.1 relating sharpness to the norm of the weight.

A finite set S ⊂ Rd is a set of extreme points if and only if for any x ∈ S, x is a vertex of the convex hull of S.

A D-layer network is a memorizing solution for a training dataset if (1) the network interpolates the training dataset, and (2) for any depth k ∈ [D − 1], there is an injection from the input data to the neurons on depth k, such that the activations in layer k for each input data is a one-hot vector with the non-zero entry being the corresponding neuron.

A D-layer network is a memorizing solution for a training dataset if (1) the network interpolates the training dataset, and (2) for any depth k ∈ [D − 1], there is an injection from the input data to the neurons on depth k, such that the activations in layer k for each input data is a one-hot vector with the non-zero entry being the corresponding neuron.
